questions.question:
The CEO's decision to downsize the workforce was considered myopic because it sacrificed long-term stability to accommodate short-term gains. Select the most appropriate option that can replace the word 'myopic' without changing the meaning of the sentence.

A. visionary  B. shortsighted  C. progressive  D. innovative
questions.to_find: Synonym of 'myopic' that preserves the sentence meaning

understand.plain: The sentence criticises the CEO's decision as myopic, meaning it focused only on immediate gains at the expense of long-term health. We need the option whose meaning matches 'myopic' most closely. 'Myopic' literally means near-sighted; figuratively it means lacking foresight or considering only the short term.

keywords.explain: Literally unable to see far; figuratively lacking foresight, focused on the immediate rather than the long-term.
keywords.example: A myopic policy ignores future consequences for a quick win today.

keywords.explain: Having or showing a lack of foresight; concerned only with immediate advantage, same as myopic.
keywords.example: Cutting the training budget is shortsighted — it saves now but hurts productivity later.

keywords.explain: Thinking about or planning the future with imagination or wisdom — the opposite of myopic.
keywords.example: A visionary leader plans ten years ahead, not just next quarter.

understand.visual_alt: No figure needed; the question is purely vocabulary-based.

given.aim: Identify the word that is a synonym of 'myopic' in the context of poor foresight

terms.meaning: Near-sighted; figuratively lacking foresight or consideration of long-term consequences
terms.example: The myopic strategy saved money today but led to losses next year.
terms.connects: Central word to be replaced; all options are evaluated against its meaning

terms.meaning: Unable or unwilling to think about long-term effects; another word for myopic
terms.example: Shortsighted decisions often cause bigger problems down the line.
terms.connects: Direct synonym of myopic; matches the context of sacrificing long-term stability

terms.meaning: Thinking far into the future; having ambitious, long-term perspective
terms.example: A visionary CEO plans infrastructure for the next decade.
terms.connects: Antonym of myopic — eliminates option A

terms.meaning: Introducing or featuring new ideas or methods; creative and original
terms.example: An innovative approach to the problem surprised everyone.
terms.connects: About novelty, not about foresight or its absence — eliminates option D

given.plan: Check each option: replace 'myopic' with it and see whether the sentence retains its critical tone about lack of foresight. The correct replacement must mean 'lacking foresight / concerned only with the short term'.

steps.title: Define the target word
steps.apply: Myopic = near-sighted = figuratively lacking foresight, focused only on the short term. The sentence says the decision was myopic because it traded long-term stability for short-term gains — a negative judgement about foresight.
steps.note: The replacement must also carry a negative connotation about foresight.

steps.title: Eliminate incorrect options
steps.apply: A. visionary — means having great foresight; opposite of myopic. Eliminated. C. progressive — means favouring forward change; positive connotation, not about lack of foresight. Eliminated. D. innovative — means introducing new ideas; about novelty not foresight. Eliminated.
steps.note: All three eliminated options carry positive or neutral connotations, unlike myopic.

steps.title: Confirm the correct option
steps.apply: B. shortsighted — means lacking foresight, concerned only with the near term; a direct synonym of myopic. Substituting it: 'The CEO's decision was considered shortsighted because it sacrificed long-term stability to accommodate short-term gains.' The sentence is grammatically correct and semantically identical.
steps.note: Shortsighted and myopic are near-perfect synonyms in formal usage; both come from the literal inability to see distant objects.

questions.formula_note: This is a vocabulary question; no mathematical formula is required.

questions.question:
Despite his initial hesitation, Rehman's ____________ to contribute to the success of the project never wavered. Select the most appropriate option to complete the above sentence.

A. ambivalence  B. satisfaction  C. resolve  D. revolve
questions.to_find: The most appropriate word to fill the blank in the sentence about Rehman's unwavering commitment

understand.plain: The sentence sets up a contrast: despite initial hesitation, something 'never wavered'. This means the blank must be a quality that (a) relates to commitment or determination and (b) can logically not waver. The phrase 'never wavered' means remained firm and unchanged throughout.

keywords.explain: Firm determination to do something; strong willpower that does not change.
keywords.example: Her resolve to finish the marathon never faltered despite the pain.

keywords.explain: Having mixed or contradictory feelings about something; uncertainty or indecisiveness.
keywords.example: His ambivalence about the job offer made it hard to give a clear answer.

keywords.explain: Became unsteady or less certain; fluctuated in resolve or opinion.
keywords.example: His confidence wavered when he saw the difficulty of the exam.

understand.visual_alt: No figure needed; the question is purely vocabulary-based.

given.aim: Find the word that best completes the sentence, capturing unwavering commitment to contribute

terms.meaning: Firm determination or commitment; intention that does not weaken
terms.example: With great resolve, she finished all the tasks on time.
terms.connects: Pairs naturally with 'never wavered' — resolve is exactly what one expects not to waver

terms.meaning: State of having two opposing feelings simultaneously; indecisiveness
terms.example: His ambivalence between staying and leaving confused everyone.
terms.connects: Ambivalence already implies uncertainty; saying it never wavered would be contradictory — eliminated

terms.meaning: Feeling of pleasure from achieving a goal or meeting a need
terms.example: She felt great satisfaction after completing the project.
terms.connects: Satisfaction is a feeling, not a commitment; saying satisfaction to contribute never wavered is semantically odd — eliminated

terms.meaning: To move in a circle around a point; unrelated to commitment
terms.example: The Earth revolves around the Sun.
terms.connects: Revolve is a verb used incorrectly here as a noun — grammatically ill-formed in this context — eliminated

given.plan: Use the 'never wavered' clue: the blank must be a noun for a mental quality (like determination) that can remain firm. Test each option for grammatical and semantic fit.

steps.title: Identify the grammatical role and semantic need
steps.apply: The blank follows a possessive (Rehman's) and precedes the infinitive phrase 'to contribute', so it must be a noun. The predicate 'never wavered' requires something that can be firm or unsteady — a mental commitment or emotion.
steps.note: Grammatical structure: Rehman's [NOUN] to contribute never wavered.

steps.title: Eliminate poor options
steps.apply: A. ambivalence — means mixed feelings (already unstable by definition); saying ambivalence never wavered contradicts the word's meaning. B. satisfaction — a feeling of fulfilment, not a commitment; awkward with 'to contribute never wavered'. D. revolve — a verb, cannot fill a noun slot; also means to rotate, entirely unrelated.
steps.note: All three fail either grammatically or semantically.

steps.title: Confirm C. resolve
steps.apply: C. resolve — noun meaning firm determination. 'Rehman's resolve to contribute to the success of the project never wavered.' This is grammatically perfect and semantically fits: despite initial hesitation he remained steadfast. This matches a classic contrastive sentence pattern.
steps.note: The contrast word 'despite' signals that whatever follows should be positive/resilient, further confirming resolve.

questions.formula_note: This is a vocabulary and grammar question; no mathematical formula is required.

questions.question:
Rajiv Gandhi Khel Ratna Award was conferred ______ Mary Kom, a six-time world champion in boxing, recently in a ceremony ______ the Rashtrapati Bhawan (the President's official residence) in New Delhi.

A. with, at  B. on, in  C. on, at  D. to, at
questions.to_find: The correct pair of prepositions to complete both blanks in the sentence

understand.plain: The sentence has two blanks requiring prepositions. The first blank follows 'conferred' and precedes a person's name; the second blank follows 'ceremony' and precedes a specific building name. Getting the correct preposition after 'conferred' and the correct locative preposition for a ceremony held at a building are the two challenges.

keywords.explain: To formally present or award something to someone; the standard preposition used with 'confer' is 'on' (confer something on someone).
keywords.example: The university conferred an honorary degree on the distinguished scientist.

keywords.explain: 'At' is used for specific points or venues (at the station, at Rashtrapati Bhawan); 'in' is used for enclosed spaces or cities/countries (in Mumbai, in a hall).
keywords.example: The ceremony was held at the White House (specific venue). She lives in Washington D.C. (city).

keywords.explain: Many verbs have fixed prepositions that always accompany them; these must be memorised as set phrases.
keywords.example: Confer on, rely on, depend on, bestow upon.

understand.visual_alt: No figure needed; the question is about preposition usage.

given.aim: Choose the correct preposition pair: one for 'conferred __ person' and one for 'ceremony __ building'

terms.meaning: The fixed collocation; 'confer X on Y' means to grant or present X to Y officially
terms.example: The award was conferred on the champion.
terms.connects: Rules out 'with' (A) and 'to' (D); both the first blank options B and C use 'on'

terms.meaning: 'At' is used for specific named places, addresses, or event venues
terms.example: The signing took place at the White House.
terms.connects: Rashtrapati Bhawan is a specific building/venue, so 'at' is correct — rules out B (which uses 'in')

terms.meaning: 'In' is for larger areas, cities, countries, or when inside something
terms.example: She lives in New Delhi; the cabinet met in Parliament.
terms.connects: The ceremony is at a building (specific venue), not in a city — 'in' is incorrect for Rashtrapati Bhawan

terms.meaning: The official residence of the President of India, a specific building in New Delhi
terms.example: The Rajiv Gandhi Khel Ratna ceremony took place at Rashtrapati Bhawan.
terms.connects: Being a specific named venue, it takes 'at' (not 'in')

given.plan: Step 1: Fix the first preposition using the 'confer on' collocation rule. Step 2: Fix the second preposition using the at-for-venues vs in-for-areas rule. Combine to identify the correct option.

steps.title: Determine the preposition for 'conferred'
steps.apply: The verb 'confer' follows the fixed collocation 'confer something ON someone'. 'The award was conferred on Mary Kom'. Options using 'with' (A) and 'to' (D) are incorrect. Remaining: B (on, in) and C (on, at).
steps.note: 'Bestow upon' is also acceptable but not listed; 'confer on' is the standard form.

steps.title: Determine the preposition for the ceremony location
steps.apply: Rashtrapati Bhawan is a specific named building (a venue/address). English uses 'at' for specific venues and addresses: 'at Rashtrapati Bhawan'. 'In' would be used for a city or country (e.g., 'in New Delhi'). Therefore the second blank is 'at', not 'in'. Option B (on, in) is eliminated.
steps.note: 'In the Rashtrapati Bhawan' would imply inside the building in a general sense; 'at Rashtrapati Bhawan' is the standard for an event at a named place.

steps.title: Select the answer
steps.apply: First blank = 'on' (from step 1), second blank = 'at' (from step 2). This matches option C: on, at.
steps.note: The actual ceremony was held at Rashtrapati Bhawan in August 2020, confirming real-world usage.

questions.formula_note: This is a preposition-collocation question; no mathematical formula is required.

questions.question:
Despite a string of poor performances, the chances of X_i Rahul's selection in the team are ________.

A. slim  B. bright  C. obvious  D. uncertain
questions.to_find: The most appropriate word to fill the blank regarding Rahul's selection chances given the context

understand.plain: The sentence uses the contrast word 'despite', signalling that the blank should give a result that is opposite to or surprising given 'poor performances'. If performances were poor, one might expect chances to be slim — but 'despite' tells us the reality is the opposite. The blank must therefore describe good or strong chances.

keywords.explain: A contrast conjunction meaning 'in spite of'; introduces a factor that one might expect to cause a certain outcome, then the main clause reverses that expectation.
keywords.example: Despite the rain, the match continued — rain suggests cancellation but the match went on.

keywords.explain: When used with 'chances' or 'prospects', bright means promising, favourable, or likely to succeed.
keywords.example: His bright prospects got him the scholarship despite average grades.

keywords.explain: Slim chances mean a low probability; used when success is unlikely.
keywords.example: There is only a slim chance of rain today.

understand.visual_alt: No figure needed; the question tests vocabulary and logical contrast.

given.aim: Identify the word that correctly uses the contrast set up by 'despite poor performances' to describe selection chances

terms.meaning: Signals that the clause following it contradicts what the first clause might imply
terms.example: Despite failing twice, he succeeded on the third attempt.
terms.connects: Poor performances imply low chances, so 'despite' flips this to mean chances are actually good

terms.meaning: Favourable, promising; used idiomatically with 'chances' or 'prospects'
terms.example: The bright chances of promotion motivated him.
terms.connects: Opposite of what poor performance suggests; correct answer given the 'despite' contrast

terms.meaning: Small or minimal; describes low probability
terms.example: There is only a slim chance of winning the lottery.
terms.connects: Would agree with poor performances (no contrast) — wrong; 'despite' requires the opposite

terms.meaning: Not known or not definite; doubtful
terms.example: The outcome of the election remains uncertain.
terms.connects: Neutral or negative; does not create the positive contrast that 'despite' demands here

given.plan: Recognise the contrast created by 'despite', then select the option that makes the result positive/surprising relative to poor performance. Eliminate options that align with or are neutral to poor performance.

steps.title: Decode the sentence structure
steps.apply: Structure: 'Despite [negative factor], the chances of selection are [blank].' The word 'despite' introduces a concessive clause — the main clause outcome should run counter to the negative factor. Poor performances normally imply low or slim chances. So the blank should give an unexpected, positive outcome.
steps.note: This is a contrast-word vocabulary pattern common in GATE aptitude questions.

steps.title: Evaluate each option
steps.apply: A. slim — low probability; aligns with poor performances, so no contrast is achieved. Eliminated. C. obvious — means clear or self-evident; does not relate to probability or success. Odd in this context. Eliminated. D. uncertain — means unclear probability; neutral, not a positive contrast. Eliminated. B. bright — means favourable/promising; directly contrasts poor performances with good chances. Correct.
steps.note: 'Bright chances' and 'bright prospects' are standard English idioms.

steps.title: Verify with substitution
steps.apply: 'Despite a string of poor performances, the chances of Rahul's selection in the team are bright.' — This reads naturally: his chances are surprisingly good despite poor form, perhaps due to lack of better alternatives or other factors. The sentence is coherent and logically uses the contrast.
steps.note: In cricket/sports commentary, 'bright chances of selection' is a standard phrase.

questions.formula_note: This is a vocabulary and logical-contrast question; no mathematical formula is required.

questions.question:
It is a common criticism that most of the academicians live in their ________, so, they are not aware of their real life challenges.

A. homes  B. ivory towers  C. glass palaces  D. big flats
questions.to_find: The correct idiomatic phrase that means being isolated from real-world challenges

understand.plain: The sentence criticises academicians for being cut off from real-life challenges. This is a well-known idiom — 'living in an ivory tower' means being isolated from the practical realities of life, typically used to criticise academics, theorists, or privileged people who are unaware of everyday problems.

keywords.explain: An idiom describing a state of privileged seclusion or separation from the practical realities of everyday life; commonly applied to academia.
keywords.example: Critics accused the philosopher of living in an ivory tower, making theories that had no practical use.

keywords.explain: A phrase whose meaning cannot be inferred from the literal definitions of its words; must be understood as a fixed expression.
keywords.example: 'Bite the bullet' means to endure a painful situation, not literally bite a bullet.

keywords.explain: Not a standard English idiom; may suggest grandeur or fragility but is not used idiomatically to mean isolation from reality.
keywords.example: The phrase 'glass palace' is rarely used in English idioms.

understand.visual_alt: No figure needed; the question tests knowledge of English idioms.

given.aim: Identify the standard English idiom for intellectual or social isolation from real-world concerns

terms.meaning: A place or situation in which someone is isolated from difficulties and realities of everyday life; used especially of academics
terms.example: Professors in their ivory towers rarely interact with industry professionals.
terms.connects: Matches the sentence context perfectly: academicians isolated from real-life challenges

terms.meaning: Literal residences; not an idiom for isolation
terms.example: Most people prefer to work from their homes.
terms.connects: Literal meaning does not capture the idea of being cut off from real-world awareness — eliminated

terms.meaning: Not a recognised English idiom; evokes luxury but not intellectual isolation
terms.example: N/A — not a standard idiom.
terms.connects: Not a recognised phrase for intellectual isolation — eliminated

terms.meaning: Literal large apartments; has no idiomatic sense
terms.example: He moved to a big flat in the city.
terms.connects: No idiomatic relevance to being unaware of real-life challenges — eliminated

given.plan: Recognise that the sentence is about intellectual or social isolation from reality — a context that requires a fixed idiom. Test whether any of the options is a standard idiom for that concept.

steps.title: Identify the context of the blank
steps.apply: The sentence criticises academicians for being isolated from real-life challenges. The blank is a place (after 'live in their') and represents their world of isolation. A literal home would not explain unawareness of real challenges. The blank must be an idiom.
steps.note: The sentence structure 'live in their ___' combined with the consequence 'not aware of real-life challenges' is the classic ivory-tower criticism.

steps.title: Eliminate non-idiomatic options
steps.apply: A. homes — literal, no idiomatic meaning of isolation. C. glass palaces — not a standard English idiom; not commonly used to mean intellectual isolation. D. big flats — purely literal, no idiomatic meaning.
steps.note: English idioms for isolation/privilege: ivory tower, bubble, cocoon — only 'ivory towers' appears among the options.

steps.title: Confirm B. ivory towers
steps.apply: The idiom 'ivory tower' was coined in the 19th century and refers to the cloistered, detached world of scholarship. Saying academicians 'live in their ivory towers' directly conveys that they are cut off from real-world problems — perfectly matching the sentence's meaning.
steps.note: The idiom is particularly apt because the sentence specifically targets academicians (scholars/academics), who are the classic subjects of this idiom.

questions.formula_note: This is an idiom-recognition question; no mathematical formula is required.

questions.question:
His hunger for reading is insatiable. He reads indiscriminately. He is most certainly a/an ______ reader.

A. all-round  B. precocious  C. voracious  D. wise
questions.to_find: The adjective that describes a reader with an insatiable, indiscriminate appetite for reading

understand.plain: Two clues are given: (1) his hunger for reading is insatiable — he never has enough; (2) he reads indiscriminately — he reads everything, without selectivity. Together, these clues describe someone who reads compulsively and in large quantities across all types of material, which is precisely what 'voracious' means.

keywords.explain: Having a very eager approach to an activity, especially eating or reading; consuming or reading in large quantities with great enthusiasm.
keywords.example: She is a voracious reader, finishing three books every week on every topic imaginable.

keywords.explain: Impossible to satisfy; always wanting more.
keywords.example: His insatiable curiosity led him to read encyclopedias for fun.

keywords.explain: Without careful judgement or selection; reading everything without distinguishing between types, quality, or genre.
keywords.example: She reads indiscriminately — novels, textbooks, magazines, instruction manuals.

understand.visual_alt: No figure needed; the question is purely vocabulary-based.

given.aim: Match the described reading behaviour (insatiable + indiscriminate) to the correct adjective

terms.meaning: Wanting or devouring great quantities; used metaphorically of reading or learning to mean consuming in large amounts
terms.example: A voracious reader reads all genres and never feels satisfied.
terms.connects: Directly matches 'insatiable hunger for reading' + 'indiscriminate reading'

terms.meaning: Having developed certain abilities or inclinations at an earlier age than usual
terms.example: A precocious child who could read at age 3.
terms.connects: About early development, not quantity or indiscriminate habit — eliminated

terms.meaning: Having many different skills or interests; versatile
terms.example: She is an all-round athlete, excelling in swimming, running, and cycling.
terms.connects: Means versatility, not insatiable appetite or indiscriminate consumption — eliminated

terms.meaning: Having or showing experience, knowledge, and good judgement
terms.example: A wise decision saves time and effort.
terms.connects: Implies selective, discerning judgement — contradicts 'indiscriminate' — eliminated

given.plan: Use both clues: insatiable (quantity/appetite) and indiscriminate (no selectivity). The correct word must mean both large-quantity consumption and non-selective reading. Check each option against these two constraints.

steps.title: Extract the key clues
steps.apply: Clue 1: 'insatiable hunger for reading' — the reader is never satisfied, always wants more. This points to quantity/appetite. Clue 2: 'reads indiscriminately' — no selectivity, reads everything. The blank must describe these combined traits.
steps.note: Both clues must be satisfied by the chosen word.

steps.title: Test each option against the clues
steps.apply: A. all-round — means versatile in many areas; does not capture insatiable appetite for reading specifically. Eliminated. B. precocious — means advanced for one's age; nothing about quantity or indiscrimination. Eliminated. D. wise — implies discerning and selective; actually contradicts 'indiscriminate'. Eliminated. C. voracious — means having a huge appetite (literal or figurative); a voracious reader reads large amounts indiscriminately. Matches both clues.
steps.note: 'Voracious' comes from Latin 'vorax' meaning devouring; it is the standard term in English for an insatiable, compulsive reader.

steps.title: Confirm with substitution
steps.apply: 'He is most certainly a voracious reader.' — Substituting this: a voracious reader has an insatiable reading appetite and reads widely without discrimination. This matches both given clues perfectly. The sentence flows naturally.
steps.note: The phrase 'voracious reader' is a very common collocation in English.

questions.formula_note: This is a vocabulary question; no mathematical formula is required.
